title: Karl Storz Image1 S Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Camera Head Not Recognized by Hub
description: Identifies causes when the camera head is not detected by the hub, preventing image display and delaying procedures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not in use on an active patient. Move the procedure to a backup camera system if needed.

steps.title: Verify Camera Head Model Compatibility
steps.instructions: Ensure the camera head is compatible with the Image1 S hub. Incompatible models may not be recognized. Expected outcome: hub should detect the correct camera head type.

steps.title: Inspect and Secure Connections
steps.instructions: Check that the camera cable is fully seated in the camera head and hub port. Look for bent pins or debris. Proper seating ensures the hub can communicate with the camera head.

steps.title: Swap Cables or Ports
steps.instructions: Replace the camera cable with a known-good cable or try a different hub port. If the hub recognizes the camera, the issue is likely a faulty cable or port.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System
steps.instructions: Turn off the hub and camera head, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on. This can reset the communication handshake. Expected outcome: hub detects the camera head.

steps.title: Check Firmware Versions
steps.instructions: Confirm that both the camera head and hub are running compatible firmware. Mismatched firmware may prevent recognition.

steps.title: Observe Hub Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for hub LEDs or display messages indicating camera detection status. These can confirm whether the hub is actively attempting recognition.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported that the Image1 S camera head was not recognized by the hub.
CCR.Cause: Cable seating checked and swapped; hub and camera head firmware incompatible; hub LEDs indicate no handshake.
CCR.Resolution: System powered down and firmware verified; device labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: Karl Storz Image1 S Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Image quality degradation
description: Addresses causes of blurry, distorted, or low-contrast images from the camera system, ensuring optimal visualization during procedures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not in use on an active patient. Move the procedure to a backup camera system if necessary. Why: Prevents patient risk while troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Camera Head and Lens
steps.instructions: Inspect the camera head lens and light guide for debris, condensation, or scratches. Clean with approved lens wipes. Expected Outcome: Clear lens improves image quality.

steps.title: Verify Cable Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure camera head, coupler, and hub connections are secure. Disconnect and reconnect each cable. Expected Outcome: Resolves signal issues that can cause image artifacts or flickering.

steps.title: Check Light Source Intensity
steps.instructions: Confirm the light source is functioning at proper intensity. Adjust settings as needed. Why: Insufficient illumination often causes low-contrast or dark images.

steps.title: Inspect Hub / Processor Settings
steps.instructions: Verify image processing modes (HD/SD, color settings, brightness, contrast) are correct for the procedure. Expected Outcome: Correct settings restore normal image appearance.

steps.title: Swap Cables or Camera Head if Available
steps.instructions: Use a known-good camera head and cables to test image quality. Expected Outcome: Determines if the issue is with the camera head, hub, or cabling.

steps.title: Restart System
steps.instructions: Power down the hub, camera head, and light source. Wait 30 seconds and power back on. Expected Outcome: Resolves temporary glitches in the imaging system.

CCR.Complaint: Surgeon reports blurry and low-contrast image during laparoscopic procedure.
CCR.Cause: Camera head lens slightly contaminated and light source intensity below standard.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned lens, verified light source, adjusted hub image settings; image quality restored.

helpfulDetails: Image quality after each troubleshooting step

title: Karl Storz Image1 S Endoscopic / OR Camera System - White Balance Failure
description: Addresses incorrect color rendering or unnatural image hues, ensuring accurate tissue visualization during procedures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not in use on an active patient. Move the procedure to a backup camera system to prevent patient risk.

steps.title: Inspect Camera Head and Hub Connections
steps.instructions: Check that the camera head is securely attached to the hub. Verify that all locking mechanisms click into place. Ensure no visible damage or debris on connectors. Expected Outcome: Properly seated connections often restore white balance automatically.

steps.title: Verify Light Source Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the LED or Xenon light source is on and functioning. Confirm the brightness and color temperature settings are within recommended ranges. Why: White balance depends on consistent, appropriate illumination.

steps.title: Perform White Balance Procedure
steps.instructions: Use the system’s white balance function, typically via the on-screen menu. Place the camera on a neutral reference target (white or gray card) and initiate calibration. Expected Outcome: Colors should appear natural and neutral; if successful, image quality is restored.

steps.title: Check for Accessories Affecting Color
steps.instructions: Remove any filters, adapters, or scope couplers that may alter light or color transmission. Why: Added optics can distort color reproduction and affect white balance.

steps.title: Inspect Cables for Damage or Interference
steps.instructions: Swap video and power cables with known-good cables. Check for kinks, frays, or bent pins. Expected Outcome: Resolves color distortion caused by poor signal transmission.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported images appearing unusually red and white tones inaccurate.
CCR.Cause: White balance calibration had drifted due to repeated usage; no physical damage found.
CCR.Resolution: Performed full white balance procedure using neutral reference target; verified natural color display.

helpfulDetails: Hub and camera head connections confirmed
Light source verified and set correctly

title: Karl Storz Image1 S Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Light Source Errors
description: Addresses low illumination, flickering, or LED failure to ensure consistent and safe visualization during procedures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not in use on an active patient. Move the procedure to a backup light source or camera system to prevent risk.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the light source unit is properly connected to a live outlet. Confirm power switch is on. Expected Outcome: Unit powers on without error indicators.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Connections
steps.instructions: Examine fiber optic or light cable for damage, kinks, or loose connections. Ensure connectors are fully seated on both camera and light source. Expected Outcome: Properly seated cables restore normal illumination.

steps.title: Check Lamp or LED Status
steps.instructions: Observe any LED indicators or display messages on the light source. Replace the lamp or LED module if indicated by the system. Expected Outcome: Light illuminates steadily without flickering.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Light Source
steps.instructions: Connect the camera head to a verified working light source unit. Expected Outcome: If illumination returns, original light source may be defective.

steps.title: Verify Settings and Mode
steps.instructions: Ensure brightness and mode settings are correctly configured on the camera and light source controller. Expected Outcome: Properly configured settings restore adequate light output.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported flickering or dim illumination from the Image1 S light source.
CCR.Cause: Fiber optic cable kinked and LED module nearing end of life.
CCR.Resolution: Straightened cable, replaced LED module, verified stable illumination.

title: Karl Storz Image1 S Endoscopic / OR Camera System - HDMI / SDI Output Failure
description: Addresses loss of video signal or corrupted output over HDMI/SDI for surgical monitors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not connected to an active procedure. Move the patient to a backup display or camera system to prevent procedural risk.

steps.title: Verify Output Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect HDMI/SDI cables for visible damage, kinks, or loose connectors. Confirm the correct input is selected on the surgical monitor. Expected outcome: Properly seated and undamaged cables should allow signal transmission.

steps.title: Test with Alternate Cables and Monitors
steps.instructions: Swap the HDMI/SDI cable with a known working one. Connect to a different compatible monitor if available. Expected outcome: If video appears, the issue is likely cable or monitor-related.

steps.title: Check Camera Hub/Processor Output Settings
steps.instructions: Access the Image1 S output settings menu. Ensure HDMI/SDI output is enabled and set to the correct resolution/format for the connected monitor. Expected outcome: Correct settings restore video output.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System
steps.instructions: Turn off the camera system and monitor. Wait 30 seconds, then power them back on. Expected outcome: Temporary glitches may clear and signal restored.

steps.title: Inspect for External Interference
steps.instructions: Ensure no high-power devices or long cable runs causing signal degradation. Expected outcome: Reducing interference may restore stable video.

CCR.Complaint: HDMI output not displaying on OR monitor during procedure.
CCR.Cause: HDMI cable was damaged; output settings on processor were misconfigured.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced cable, corrected output settings, verified video on monitor.

helpfulDetails: Monitor model and input port used
Any unusual video artifacts, flicker, or color distortion

title: Karl Storz Xenon Nova 300 Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Lamp Not Igniting or Low Output
description: Lamp not turning on or producing weak illumination due to power, connection, lamp life, or external system setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the light source fails during a procedure, immediately transition to a backup light source or system. Expected: Procedure continues safely with adequate visualization. Why it matters: Loss of illumination eliminates visualization and creates immediate patient risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and observe behavior: No light output at all, light attempts to start but fails, dim or flickering illumination. Check for any status indicators, alarms, or fan activity. Expected: Clear identification of whether the issue is no ignition vs low output. Why it matters: Helps differentiate between lamp failure and system/setup issues.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Unit Status (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Confirm unit is plugged into a known-good outlet. Verify power switch is ON. Observe front panel indicators or display. Test outlet if needed. Expected: Unit powers on with normal indicators. Why it matters: No lamp ignition can simply be a power issue.

steps.title: Confirm Light Source is Enabled
steps.instructions: Ensure the light output is not set to standby or minimum. Adjust brightness/output control. Verify system is not waiting for scope connection (if applicable setup). Expected: Lamp attempts ignition when output is increased. Why it matters: Some systems will not activate lamp without proper enable conditions.

steps.title: Inspect Light Cable and Scope Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure light cable is fully seated in the light source port. Check for proper locking/engagement. Inspect cable for damage (burn marks, cracks, discoloration). Confirm proper connection to scope or camera head. Expected: Secure, undamaged connection. Why it matters: Poor coupling reduces or blocks light transmission.

steps.title: Check Light Cable for Damage or Degradation
steps.instructions: Swap with a known-good light cable if available. Expected: Normal brightness restored with alternate cable. Why it matters: Fiber optic cables degrade over time, causing low output.

steps.title: Evaluate Lamp Life / Usage Hours (Very Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Check lamp hour counter if available. Determine if lamp is near or beyond expected life. Expected: Lamp within usable life range. Why it matters: Xenon lamps lose intensity before complete failure.

steps.title: Attempt Lamp Re-Ignition Cycle
steps.instructions: Power the unit OFF. Wait 30–60 seconds. Power ON and attempt ignition again. Expected: Lamp ignites normally. Why it matters: Xenon lamps sometimes require a reset cycle to ignite.

steps.title: Observe for Cooling or Fan Issues
steps.instructions: Listen for internal fan operation. Check for overheating indicators or shutdown behavior. Expected: Fan running normally with no over-temp warnings. Why it matters: Overheating can prevent lamp ignition or reduce output.

CCR.Complaint: Light source not producing illumination during endoscopic procedure.
CCR.Cause: Xenon lamp at end-of-life with degraded output.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced lamp module and verified normal light output.

helpfulDetails: Indicator lights and fan behavior observed
Any flickering or delayed ignition noted
Final device status (operational or sent for repair)

title: Karl Storz Xenon Nova 300 Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Scope Port Recognition Failure
description: Scope not recognized by the light source due to connection issues, damaged ports, incompatible adapters, or external accessory faults affecting illumination and system communication.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the scope is not recognized during a procedure, stop use and transition to a backup light source/system.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the Xenon Nova 300 and connect the scope. Observe for no light output, no response, or intermittent recognition.

steps.title: Check Scope Connection at Light Source (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect the scope light cable. Ensure connector is fully seated and properly aligned.

steps.title: Inspect Scope Light Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Check for bent pins, cracked housing, or contamination affecting connection integrity.

steps.title: Verify Correct Adapter or Light Cable Compatibility
steps.instructions: Ensure correct Karl Storz-compatible cable is used and remove third-party adapters if present.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Scope or Light Cable
steps.instructions: Swap components individually to determine if the issue follows the cable or scope.

steps.title: Inspect Light Source Port for Damage or Debris
steps.instructions: Check for bent contacts, debris, or signs of wear inside the port.

steps.title: Check Light Source Status Indicators and Functionality
steps.instructions: Observe startup sequence, fan operation, and any error indicators.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle and Reinitialize Connection
steps.instructions: Power off, disconnect power for 30 seconds, restart, and reconnect the scope.

CCR.Complaint: Light source not recognizing scope; no illumination during setup.
CCR.Cause: Fault traced to damaged light cable connector preventing proper interface.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced light cable and verified normal operation with proper illumination.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Karl Storz Xenon Nova 300 Light Source - Fan Failure or Overtemperature Alarm
description: Troubleshooting fan failure or overheating alarms caused by airflow restrictions, environmental conditions, or cooling system faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the alarm occurs during a procedure, notify clinical staff and transition to a backup light source.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and confirm the exact alarm message or indicator including fan failure messages, overtemperature warnings, audible alarms, and reduced or shut-off light output.

steps.title: Check Ventilation Openings (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Inspect intake and exhaust vents, remove dust or debris, and ensure vents are not blocked by drapes, walls, or equipment.

steps.title: Verify Proper Placement and Clearance
steps.instructions: Ensure several inches of clearance around the unit and avoid enclosed cabinets or tightly stacked equipment.

steps.title: Check Ambient Room Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify room temperature is within normal range and HVAC airflow is present and functional.

steps.title: Listen for Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and listen for normal fan operation or abnormal sounds such as grinding, rattling, or no sound.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 30–60 seconds, then power it back on to clear potential temporary thermal conditions.

steps.title: Check for External Obstructions or Foreign Objects
steps.instructions: Inspect vent areas for debris such as tape or dust buildup and remove any visible obstructions safely.

steps.title: Evaluate Usage Duration and Load
steps.instructions: Determine if the unit has been running continuously and allow it to cool down fully before reuse if overheated.

CCR.Complaint: Light source alarming with fan failure and shutting down during use.
CCR.Cause: Internal cooling fan not operating despite proper external airflow and placement.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair; provided backup unit.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (removed from service or returned to use)

title: Karl Storz Xenon Nova 300 Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Footswitch or Remote Not Responding
description: Footswitch or remote control not activating light functions due to connection issues, incorrect settings, or accessory faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the footswitch or remote is required during a procedure, notify staff and transition to manual control or backup equipment. Ensure the procedure continues safely without interruption. Loss of control can delay adjustments during critical visualization.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to activate the light source using the footswitch or remote. Observe for no response, intermittent response, or delayed activation. Confirm the issue is reproducible. Confirms this is not user technique or timing-related.

steps.title: Check Footswitch/Remote Connection (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Inspect the cable connection at the light source. Ensure connector is fully seated. Disconnect and reconnect firmly. Check for bent pins or loose connectors. Loose or misaligned connections commonly prevent signal transmission.

steps.title: Inspect Cable and Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Check the entire length of the footswitch or remote cable. Look for cuts, fraying, or crushed sections. Inspect connector ends for damage. Damaged wiring interrupts control signals.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Port Usage
steps.instructions: Ensure the footswitch or remote is plugged into the correct control port on the unit. Incorrect port placement results in no response.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Footswitch or Remote
steps.instructions: Swap with a known working accessory if available. Determine if the replacement works correctly. Isolates whether the issue is accessory-related or system-related.

steps.title: Check System Settings (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Review system configuration for remote or footswitch enablement. Confirm control input is enabled. Check for mode settings that disable external control. Some configurations can disable external inputs.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Light Source
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off. Wait 10–15 seconds. Power back on. Clears temporary communication faults.

steps.title: Observe for Additional Symptoms
steps.instructions: Check for no response from all external controls. Look for error indicators or warning lights. Monitor for unusual system behavior. Multiple failures may indicate internal control board issues.

CCR.Complaint: Footswitch not activating light source during endoscopic procedure.
CCR.Cause: Failed footswitch cable identified after testing with known good unit.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced defective footswitch and verified proper operation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or lack of response from controls
Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Karl Storz Xenon Nova 300 Endoscopic / OR Camera System - Power Supply or Power-On Failure
description: No power, no startup response, or intermittent power issues caused by external power sources, connections, or basic system faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the light source fails during a procedure, immediately transition to a backup light source or system. Expected: Procedure continues safely with adequate visualization Why it matters: Loss of illumination directly impacts visibility and patient safety

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the unit and observe behavior: No lights, no fan, no response Brief power then shutdown Intermittent or delayed startup Expected: Clear identification of power failure type Why it matters: Helps determine if issue is input power, internal protection, or load-related

steps.title: Check AC Power Source (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Verify outlet functionality using a known-good device. Ensure connection to a hospital-grade outlet or approved power strip. Expected: Confirmed stable power source Why it matters: Dead or unstable outlets are a frequent cause of no-power conditions

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Connections
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for: Loose connections at wall or device Visible damage or fraying Reseat both ends securely. Replace with a known-good cord if available. Expected: Secure, intact power connection Why it matters: Damaged or loose cords prevent proper power delivery

steps.title: Verify Rear Power Switch and Fuse Area
steps.instructions: Ensure the main power switch (if present) is in the ON position. Inspect the fuse holder: Check for blown fuse Reseat or replace with correct rating if needed Expected: Unit receives power after fuse or switch correction Why it matters: Blown fuses commonly occur after power surges or faults

steps.title: Disconnect Accessories and Scope
steps.instructions: Remove all connected components: Light cable Scope Any external control or video system connections Attempt to power on with only AC power connected. Expected: Unit powers on if external load was causing issue Why it matters: Shorted accessories can prevent startup or trigger protection shutdown

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Thermal Protection
steps.instructions: Feel for excessive heat or listen for fan inactivity. Allow the unit to cool for 15–20 minutes and retry. Expected: Unit powers on after cooling if thermal protection was triggered Why it matters: Overheating can cause automatic shutdown or prevent startup

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights and Fan Behavior
steps.instructions: During power attempt, note: Any LED activity Fan spin or lack of movement Clicking or abnormal sounds Expected: Some response indicates partial power or internal fault Why it matters: Helps differentiate complete power failure vs internal subsystem issue

CCR.Complaint: Light source would not power on during OR setup.
CCR.Cause: Failed AC power cord connection at device inlet.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated power cord and verified normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual heat, smell, or sound
Final device status (operational or removed from service)

title: Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor Video Laryngoscope - No Display / Monitor Does Not Power On
description: Troubleshooting a C-MAC HD Monitor with a blank screen or no power caused by battery, external power, connection, or physical damage.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-MAC HD Monitor from clinical use if it does not power on or the display cannot be confirmed functional. If it is needed for an active or anticipated airway procedure, provide clinical staff with a known-working video laryngoscope or appropriate backup airway visualization device before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on unavailable or unverified airway visualization equipment. Why it matters: A monitor that does not power on cannot provide dependable visualization during airway management.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: With the device away from patient care, press the monitor power control normally and observe for: Display illumination or startup screen; Battery or charging indication; Indicator light activity; Audible startup response; Intermittent power when the unit is repositioned. Document whether the monitor is completely unresponsive or whether it briefly starts and then shuts down. Expected outcome: The failure is verified as a true no-power or no-display condition rather than an image-source problem. If the monitor powers on but displays no laryngoscope image, stop this workflow and evaluate the connected video laryngoscope, cable, and video input path instead.

steps.title: 3. Inspect the Monitor for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Examine the monitor housing, screen, power control area, charging/power connection, and battery area for: Cracks, impact damage, or fluid intrusion evidence; Bent, contaminated, recessed, or loose connector contacts; Damaged power button or controls; Swelling, heat, odor, or damage involving the battery area. Do not attempt to energize a device with suspected fluid intrusion, battery swelling, unusual heat, or burning odor. Expected outcome: The monitor is either physically safe to continue evaluating or immediately removed from service due to visible damage.

steps.title: 4. Check Battery Charge Status and External Power Operation
steps.instructions: The C-MAC HD Monitor uses a rechargeable lithium-ion battery and has a 12 V input specification. Verify whether the unit powers on from its approved external power supply or charging setup. Actions: Connect the approved Karl Storz power supply to the monitor; Connect the supply to a known-working receptacle; Observe for any charging, battery, or power indication; Allow sufficient charging time per department practice or approved manufacturer guidance before retesting power-on. Expected outcome: If the monitor powers on with external power, suspect a depleted, failed, or poorly charging battery condition. If the monitor remains completely unresponsive, continue troubleshooting. If restoring approved external power resolves the problem and the battery subsequently charges and supports normal operation, document the resolution and stop.

steps.title: 5. Verify the Wall Outlet and External Power Supply Path
steps.instructions: Check the simplest external power causes before suspecting monitor failure. Actions: Test the receptacle with approved equipment or a receptacle tester according to facility practice; Inspect the power supply cord and output cable for cuts, strain damage, bent pins, loose connectors, or evidence of overheating; Reseat all accessible power connections firmly; When available, substitute a known-good compatible C-MAC monitor power supply approved for use with this equipment. Expected outcome: The monitor receives confirmed external power through an intact power path. If a known-good approved power supply restores operation, remove the defective power supply from service, verify normal monitor function, document the repair, and stop.

steps.title: 6. Isolate Accessories and Connected Components
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential attached items, such as connected video laryngoscopes, external display cables, USB devices, or SD media, as applicable to the configuration. Attempt to power on the monitor using only its approved power source. Expected outcome: The monitor starts without an attached accessory contributing to the failure. If the monitor powers on only after an accessory is removed, inspect and isolate the suspect accessory or cable before returning the system to service.

steps.title: 7. Evaluate for Intermittent Power Behavior
steps.instructions: While the monitor is safely positioned on the bench and not in clinical use, observe whether power indication changes when: The external power connector is gently checked for secure seating; The approved power supply cable is placed in a neutral, unstressed position; The monitor is allowed to remain powered on without movement. Do not flex connectors excessively or attempt to reproduce a fault in a way that could further damage the unit. Expected outcome: Power remains stable without flickering, unexpected shutdown, or loss of charging indication. If power is intermittent at the monitor connector, battery area, or control surface, remove the monitor from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 8. Perform a Safe Functional Verification if Power Is Restored
steps.instructions: If the monitor powers on after external checks: Confirm the display illuminates normally and remains stable; Confirm the battery or external-power indication behaves normally; Connect a known-good compatible video laryngoscope, when available; Verify that a stable image is displayed; Confirm the unit remains powered during a brief unoccupied functional check. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on reliably and displays a stable video image without interruption. If all checks pass, the monitor may be returned to service according to department policy.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor screen remained blank and the unit would not power on when needed for airway equipment setup.
CCR.Cause: Monitor remained unresponsive with a verified working outlet and known-good approved external power supply, with no recovery after accessory isolation and charging attempt.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-MAC HD Monitor from service, labeled it Out of Service, provided a replacement monitor for clinical use, and routed the failed unit for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Approved power supply inspected or swapped
Any fluid intrusion, heat, swelling, or unusual odor
Whether the monitor powered on under external power
Whether a known-good laryngoscope produced an image after recovery
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair

title: Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor Video Laryngoscope - Battery Will Not Charge or Rapidly Depletes
description: Troubleshooting charging failure, short battery runtime, incorrect charge indication, power-supply issues, or a degraded internal battery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-MAC HD Monitor from clinical use if the battery cannot be trusted to support airway visualization during a procedure. If the device is needed for an active or anticipated airway intervention, provide a known-working video laryngoscope or approved backup airway visualization method before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Clinical staff are not dependent on a monitor that may lose power unexpectedly. Why it matters: Unexpected monitor shutdown during intubation may interrupt visualization at a critical time.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Battery Behavior
steps.instructions: With the monitor removed from patient use, confirm the reported issue: Battery does not appear to charge. Battery capacity indicator remains low after charging. Monitor operates on external power but shuts down quickly on battery. Monitor powers off unexpectedly during battery operation. Battery runtime is significantly shorter than expected. Document the battery indicator behavior, whether the unit functions on external power, and the approximate observed runtime. Expected outcome: The complaint is confirmed and separated into charging failure, battery-runtime failure, or both. Why it matters: The C-MAC HD Monitor uses a rechargeable lithium-ion battery with a displayed capacity indicator; current Karl Storz product information lists a maximum battery operating time of 120 minutes for the C-MAC HD Monitor.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor and Power Input Externally
steps.instructions: Examine the monitor housing and external power-input area for: Cracks, impact damage, or signs of fluid ingress. Loose, bent, recessed, or contaminated power connections. Unusual heat, odor, swelling, or discoloration. Damage to the power supply cable, connector, strain relief, or adapter body. Do not continue charging or operating the device if there is swelling, unusual heat, burning odor, or visible electrical damage. Expected outcome: The monitor and charging connection are free of obvious physical or electrical safety concerns. If damage is found: Stop troubleshooting, remove the monitor from service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Confirm the Outlet and External Power Source
steps.instructions: Connect the monitor power supply to a known-working receptacle. Verify the outlet using approved Clinical Engineering methods or by confirming operation of another appropriate device. Observe whether the monitor responds to external power and whether the charging indication appears. Expected outcome: The unit recognizes external power and begins indicating charging or available external-power operation. If the issue resolves: The original receptacle or power source was the likely cause. Document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Approved Power Supply Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm that the power supply is the correct Karl Storz-approved supply intended for the C-MAC HD Monitor and that its connector seats fully without force or looseness. Do not substitute an unapproved adapter simply because the connector fits. Expected outcome: The monitor is being evaluated with the correct power accessory and a secure connection. Why it matters: The C-MAC HD Monitor technical specification identifies a 12 V input, and use of an incorrect supply may prevent proper charging or damage the equipment.

steps.title: Compare Operation on External Power Versus Battery Power
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor on while connected to the approved external power supply. Observe whether it: Boots normally. Remains powered without interruption. Displays a charging or battery-capacity response. Then disconnect external power after an appropriate charge attempt and observe whether the monitor continues operating on battery. Expected outcome: The device operates normally on external power and maintains operation when transferred to battery use. Interpretation: Does not operate on external power: Suspect power supply, power-input, or internal power-system failure. Operates on external power but not battery: Suspect depleted, failed, or non-charging battery condition. Operates briefly on battery and rapidly shuts down: Suspect degraded battery capacity or internal battery-management fault.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Charging at the Connector
steps.instructions: With the monitor safely positioned on a bench and connected to approved external power, gently observe the cable connection without aggressively bending or stressing it. Document whether the charge indication or power state changes when the cable is lightly repositioned. Expected outcome: Charging and external-power operation remain stable without interruption. If charging is intermittent: Remove the monitor and associated power supply from service for evaluation of the connector, cable, or internal power input.

steps.title: Allow an Appropriate Charge Period and Reassess Runtime
steps.instructions: Using the approved power supply and a verified outlet, allow the monitor to charge according to facility practice and manufacturer instructions. After charging, operate the monitor off external power in a controlled, non-clinical setting and observe: Battery capacity indication. Whether charge level drops unusually quickly. Whether the monitor unexpectedly shuts down. Approximate operating time before low-battery indication or shutdown. Expected outcome: Battery runtime is reasonably consistent with normal use and does not rapidly collapse. If the issue resolves: The monitor may have been deeply discharged or previously connected to an unreliable power source. Return to service only after satisfactory functional verification.

steps.title: Substitute a Known-Good Approved Power Supply, If Available
steps.instructions: When available, test the monitor with a known-good, approved Karl Storz power supply intended for the same monitor configuration. Do not open the monitor or attempt internal battery replacement as part of floor-level troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The monitor charges normally with the known-good power supply. Interpretation: Charges normally with substitute supply: Remove the failed power supply from service and replace it. Still will not charge or rapidly depletes: The monitor likely requires battery or internal charging-system evaluation.

steps.title: Complete a Final Functional Check Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: Return the device to service only if: The monitor accepts external power reliably. Charging indication behaves normally. Battery operation is stable. No abnormal heat, odor, or physical damage is present. The monitor remains powered long enough to support intended clinical readiness. Expected outcome: The monitor is safe and dependable for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor would not maintain a charge and shut down shortly after being removed from external power.
CCR.Cause: Monitor operated normally on the approved external power supply, but battery capacity dropped rapidly during bench testing after charging, indicating a likely degraded internal battery or charging-system fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-MAC HD Monitor from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented failed battery runtime verification, and routed the unit for authorized battery/power-system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Approved Karl Storz power supply verified.
Substitute approved power supply tested, if available.
Intermittent behavior at the power connection.
Any unusual heat, odor, swelling, or visible damage.
Whether the unit operated normally on external power.
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair.

title: Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor Video Laryngoscope - Connected Video Laryngoscope Not Recognized / No Image
description: Troubleshooting a connected C-MAC video laryngoscope that produces no live image because of power, connection, input-selection, cable, or scope damage.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the C-MAC HD Monitor and connected video laryngoscope from clinical use when a live airway image cannot be confirmed. If the device is needed for an active or imminent airway procedure, notify clinical staff immediately and provide a known-working video laryngoscope or appropriate backup airway visualization device before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on a video laryngoscope system with an unavailable or unreliable image. Why it matters: A video laryngoscope displaying no image, the wrong image, or an intermittent image cannot be trusted during airway management.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: With the device removed from patient use, power on the C-MAC HD Monitor and connect the reported video laryngoscope. Observe and document whether: The monitor powers on normally. The display remains blank, shows a menu, or shows an image from another connected device. The image appears only intermittently when the cable or scope is moved. An error message, connection symbol, or abnormal screen indication appears. One or two video devices are connected to the monitor. Expected outcome: The reported no-image condition is reproduced and clearly characterized. Why it matters: The C-MAC HD Monitor supports two video inputs and display modes including Picture-in-Picture and Side-by-Side; an incorrectly selected input or second connected device may make a connected laryngoscope appear not recognized.

steps.title: Verify Monitor Power and Basic Display Function
steps.instructions: Check that the monitor has sufficient battery charge or is connected to its approved power supply. Confirm that the display powers on and responds to normal controls. If the monitor does not power on, has a depleted battery, or cannot maintain power, address the power condition before continuing with image troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The C-MAC HD Monitor remains powered on with a usable display. If resolved: Reconnect the video laryngoscope, confirm a stable live image, complete functional verification, and return the device to service only when safe.

steps.title: Check Video Input Selection and Display Mode
steps.instructions: The C-MAC HD Monitor has two video inputs. If two devices are attached, verify that the intended video laryngoscope input is selected for display. Check for: The wrong connected device being displayed. Picture-in-Picture or Side-by-Side mode causing confusion about the active source. A live image appearing when the correct input is selected. Disconnect the unused video device temporarily, when appropriate, and retest the reported laryngoscope by itself. Expected outcome: The connected video laryngoscope appears as the selected live video source. If resolved: Confirm stable live imaging with the correct laryngoscope and document an input-selection or display-mode issue. The C-MAC HD Monitor is designed with two video inputs and dual-source display capabilities.

steps.title: Inspect the External Connection at the Monitor
steps.instructions: Disconnect the video laryngoscope cable from the monitor and inspect the external connector and monitor input port for: Incomplete seating. Bent, pushed-in, contaminated, or damaged connector contacts. Cracked connector housing. Fluid residue or corrosion. A cable that does not lock or seat securely. Reconnect the cable carefully using the connector orientation features. Do not force the connector. Test the alternate video input on the monitor, when available and clinically appropriate. Expected outcome: The connector seats fully and securely, and a live image appears on at least one monitor input. If resolved: Document whether reseating the connector or using the alternate input restored the image. If one input remains nonfunctional, remove the monitor from service for evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the Video Laryngoscope and Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the connection between the video laryngoscope and its cable, when applicable, for: A connector that is not fully seated. Damaged cable jacket, strain relief, or connector shell. Bent pins or visible debris. Damage to the laryngoscope handle or connection socket. Evidence of fluid intrusion, impact, or improper handling. Reconnect the cable completely without forcing it, then retest for a live image. Expected outcome: The scope-side connection is secure and the image is restored without intermittent behavior. If resolved: Confirm the image remains stable during gentle normal handling before returning the system to service.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Cable or Connector Failure
steps.instructions: With the monitor powered and the system removed from clinical use, observe the image while gently repositioning the external cable near each strain-relief area and connector. Do not sharply bend, twist, or stress the cable. Look for: Image appearing and disappearing. Flickering or color distortion. Connection loss with slight movement. The monitor recognizing the device only in certain cable positions. Expected outcome: The live image remains stable during normal cable positioning. If the image is intermittent: Remove the affected cable, laryngoscope, or monitor from service as indicated. Intermittent video during airway visualization is not acceptable for clinical use.

steps.title: Isolate the Failed Component with Known-Working Accessories
steps.instructions: When available, use approved, known-working C-MAC components to isolate the cause: Connect a known-working compatible video laryngoscope to the reported monitor. Connect the reported video laryngoscope to a known-working compatible C-MAC monitor. Substitute the approved video connection cable, when the configuration uses a separate cable. Interpret the results: Known-working laryngoscope also produces no image on the reported monitor: Monitor input or monitor hardware fault is likely. Reported laryngoscope fails on a known-working monitor: Laryngoscope or its cable is likely faulty. Substitute cable restores the image: Remove the failed cable from service. All components work after reconnection: Suspect incomplete seating, incorrect input selection, or an intermittent fault; continue observation before clearing for use. Expected outcome: The faulty component is identified without unnecessary internal repair activity.

steps.title: Confirm a True Live Image Before Returning to Service
steps.instructions: Before clearing the device for clinical use, confirm that: The intended video laryngoscope is recognized by the monitor. A live image is visible immediately after connection. The displayed image changes appropriately when the scope is moved in a safe test environment. The image remains stable during gentle normal cable movement. Both monitor power and video connection remain reliable. No damaged connector, cable, or housing remains in use. Expected outcome: The system provides a stable, correct live image suitable for clinical use. If resolved: Return the device to service according to department procedure and document the findings and verification performed.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor powered on but did not display an image from the connected video laryngoscope.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the connected video laryngoscope remained without live image after reseating connections and verifying the correct video input; the reported scope also failed when tested on a known-working compatible monitor.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the affected video laryngoscope from service, labeled it Out of Service, verified the C-MAC HD Monitor displayed a stable live image with a known-working scope, and routed the failed component for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Monitor battery charge or external power status.
Whether the monitor powered on normally.
Whether one or two video devices were connected.
Display mode observed, such as single-image, Picture-in-Picture, or Side-by-Side.
Whether reseating the connector restored the image.
Condition of cables, strain reliefs, and connector contacts.
Results using a known-working laryngoscope, cable, or monitor.
Whether image loss was constant or intermittent.

title: Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor Video Laryngoscope - Intermittent Image Loss at Cable or Input Connection
description: Identifying intermittent video loss caused by loose connections, damaged interface cables, connector contamination, strain, or an unreliable monitor input.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot intermittent image loss during an active airway procedure. If the image is unstable during use, clinical staff should immediately transition to an alternate airway visualization method or known-working video laryngoscope system according to clinical need. Remove the affected C-MAC HD Monitor and connected accessory from patient use before testing. Expected outcome: The patient is no longer dependent on a video system with an unreliable image. Why it matters: Loss of visualization during intubation can delay airway placement and create immediate patient risk.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Image-Loss Behavior
steps.instructions: With the monitor removed from service, power it on and connect the reported C-MAC-compatible video laryngoscope or airway visualization accessory. Observe whether the image is: Completely lost and restored intermittently. Lost when the cable or connector is lightly repositioned. Stable at first but lost after normal handling. Accompanied by flickering, freezing, color distortion, or a black screen. Document which connection point appears associated with the symptom. Expected outcome: The intermittent fault is reproduced or the reported behavior is clearly documented. Why it matters: The C-MAC HD Monitor is intended to connect Karl Storz airway management visualization devices through the C-MAC platform, so identifying whether the failure follows the connected accessory, cable, or monitor interface is essential.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor Input and Cable Connection Externally
steps.instructions: Disconnect the accessory cable from the monitor and inspect the accessible connection points for: Incomplete seating or a loose-fitting connector. Bent, recessed, contaminated, or visibly damaged contacts. Cracked connector housing. Damaged strain relief. Fluid residue, debris, or evidence of improper handling. Cable pull or tension from routing, cart positioning, or storage. Reconnect the cable fully without forcing the connection. Expected outcome: The connector seats securely and the image remains stable without cable strain. If this resolves the issue: Return to functional verification and stop troubleshooting once reliable operation is confirmed.

steps.title: Check for Cable Strain or Position-Dependent Image Loss
steps.instructions: While observing the image on the monitor, gently reposition the external cable near: The monitor input connection. The cable strain-relief areas. The connected laryngoscope or visualization device connection. Do not sharply bend, twist, pull, or stress the cable. Expected outcome: The image remains continuously stable during normal, gentle handling. Abnormal finding: If the image drops out, flickers, or returns when the cable position changes, suspect a damaged cable, connector, or input interface. If abnormal: Do not return the system to clinical use based on temporary repositioning.

steps.title: Inspect the Connected Video Laryngoscope or Accessory
steps.instructions: Check the attached C-MAC-compatible video laryngoscope or other connected visualization accessory for: Connector damage. Cracked housing near the cable entry point. Contamination or moisture at accessible external connection surfaces. Evidence of impact, crushing, or repeated cable strain. Image loss associated specifically with movement at the accessory end. Expected outcome: The attached device and its connector appear intact and do not cause image loss during normal handling. Why it matters: An intermittent image-loss complaint may originate from the connected scope or accessory rather than the monitor itself.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Compatible Accessory or Cable, When Available
steps.instructions: Connect a known-working compatible Karl Storz visualization accessory or interface cable according to department procedures. Compare operation: If the monitor image becomes stable with a known-good accessory or cable, remove the suspect accessory or cable from service. If intermittent image loss continues with a known-good connection setup, suspect the monitor input or monitor-side interface. Expected outcome: The faulty component is isolated without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Verify the Monitor Remains Stable During Functional Handling
steps.instructions: With a known-good or corrected connection installed, allow the monitor to operate while gently repositioning the cable as it would be handled during setup and transport. Confirm: Continuous image display. No flickering or black screen. No loss of image when the connection is lightly handled. No visible damage or looseness at the input. Expected outcome: The image remains stable during normal handling conditions. If this resolves the issue: Document the identified external cause and the successful operational check.

steps.title: Evaluate Cleaning, Storage, and Transport Factors
steps.instructions: Review whether the cable or input connection may have been affected by: Fluid intrusion or residue after cleaning. Cable wrapped too tightly during storage. Cable pinched in a cart, drawer, or transport case. Monitor transported with the cable connected and under tension. Repeated pulling on the cable rather than the connector body. Correct storage or handling issues only after the equipment has passed functional testing. Expected outcome: Preventable external stressors are identified and addressed.

CCR.Complaint: Anesthesia reported that the Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor intermittently loses the video image when the connected cable is moved near the monitor input.
CCR.Cause: Bench testing reproduced image loss during gentle movement at the monitor-side connection, and the symptom remained present with a known-good compatible accessory, indicating a suspected monitor input/interface fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-MAC HD Monitor from service, labeled it Out of Service, and routed it for bench evaluation or repair due to unreliable video input operation.

helpfulDetails: Image-loss behavior reproduced or not reproduced.
Specific connection point associated with image loss.
Connector visually inspected for damage or contamination.
Known-good compatible accessory or cable tested.
Whether image loss occurred with gentle handling.
Evidence of fluid residue, impact, or cable pinching.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor Video Laryngoscope - SD Card, USB Export, or Image Documentation Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed image/video capture, missing recordings, SD card recognition problems, or inability to transfer stored documentation to USB.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Remove the monitor from active airway use before troubleshooting any documentation or export problem.

If the monitor is needed for an airway procedure, provide clinical staff with a known-working video laryngoscope or alternate visualization method before evaluating the affected unit.

Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed without interrupting airway management or relying on a monitor with an unresolved documentation concern.

Why it matters: Failure to capture or export documentation should never delay or interfere with patient airway management.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions:
With the unit removed from clinical use, determine exactly what failed:

Image capture does not save a still image.
Video recording does not start or save.
Previously recorded files cannot be viewed on the monitor.
SD card is not recognized.
Files are present on the monitor but will not transfer to USB.
USB device is not recognized.
Staff expected a saved file, but no file is available.

Create a test capture only using a non-patient setup.

Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to capture, storage, playback, USB recognition, or export.

If the documentation function works normally during testing: Confirm saved test files are viewable or exportable, document the findings, and return the device only according to department policy.

steps.title: Verify Basic Monitor Operation
steps.instructions:
Power the monitor on and confirm that it boots normally, displays a usable image from a connected compatible scope or imager, and responds to touchscreen or documentation controls.

Inspect the monitor for visible damage, liquid intrusion, cracked ports, bent contacts, or signs of impact near the SD card or USB interfaces.

Expected outcome: The monitor functions normally apart from the reported storage or export concern.

If the monitor has startup, display, control, or port damage: Stop troubleshooting and send the unit for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the SD Card and SD Card Slot Externally
steps.instructions:
Power down the monitor before removing or reseating the SD card.

Check that:

An SD card is installed.
The card is fully seated.
The card is not cracked, contaminated, bent, or physically damaged.
The SD card slot does not contain debris or show damaged contacts.
The card’s write-protect tab, when present, is not accidentally set to prevent writing.

Reinstall the card securely and restart the monitor.

Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the SD card and allows a test image or video to be stored.

Why it matters: The C-MAC HD Monitor records images and videos to SD card before USB transfer is possible.

If reseating the card restores recording: Confirm playback of the saved test file, document the corrective action, and stop.

steps.title: Check Available Storage and Playback
steps.instructions:
Using the monitor’s playback or stored-file function, check whether existing recordings can be accessed and whether the monitor indicates inadequate remaining storage or a storage-related warning.

When approved by department policy, protect or transfer needed files before clearing unnecessary test or non-required documentation.

Expected outcome: Adequate storage is available for a new test capture, and saved files can be viewed on the monitor.

If recordings save after storage is made available: Verify one new still image and one short test video as appropriate, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Image and Video Capture Test
steps.instructions:
Connect a known-compatible C-MAC video laryngoscope, S IMAGER, or other approved compatible source.

With no patient involved:

Confirm live image display.
Capture one still image.
Record a short test video, when applicable.
Open playback and verify the test file exists and can be viewed.

Expected outcome: The monitor saves and plays back documentation from a controlled test.

If the live image is present but no test file saves: Continue troubleshooting the SD card/storage pathway.

If no live image is present: Address the scope, imager, cable, or video input issue separately; documentation cannot be verified without a valid image source.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Approved SD Card
steps.instructions:
When permitted by local policy and manufacturer-supported accessory practices, test the monitor with a known-good compatible SD card intended for C-MAC use.

Do not use unidentified media containing patient information, and do not format or erase clinical media without authorization.

Repeat a test still-image capture and playback verification.

Expected outcome: The monitor records normally using known-good media.

If the known-good SD card resolves the issue: Remove the failed or suspect card from use, handle any stored patient information according to facility policy, replace the media as appropriate, and document the result.

If the known-good card is also not recognized or cannot save: The fault is more likely related to the monitor’s SD card interface, software, or internal documentation circuitry.

steps.title: Inspect and Test USB Export
steps.instructions:
Once files are confirmed to exist on the SD card through monitor playback, insert a known-good approved USB flash drive into the monitor’s USB port.

Check that:

The USB drive is properly inserted.
The connector is not damaged or loose.
The USB port has no visible contamination or bent components.
The monitor recognizes the USB device.
A test file can be transferred from the SD card to USB using the normal export function.

The C-MAC HD Monitor is designed for transfer of stored documentation from the SD card to a USB flash drive through its USB interface.

Expected outcome: A stored test image or video transfers successfully to USB.

If export works with the known-good USB drive: The originally used USB device may be incompatible, corrupted, full, or damaged. Document the test result and stop.

steps.title: Differentiate Capture Failure From Export Failure
steps.instructions:
Use the following logic:

Files do not save to the SD card: Focus on SD card, SD slot, storage availability, or monitor documentation function.
Files save and play back on the monitor but will not export to USB: Focus on USB media, USB port, or export function.
Files export successfully but cannot be opened on a workstation: Evaluate the USB drive, workstation permissions, file-handling process, or supported playback software outside the monitor.
Documentation controls do not respond while other monitor functions work: Treat as a monitor control/software concern requiring further evaluation.

Expected outcome: The failure is isolated to the correct part of the documentation workflow.

steps.title: Complete Final Functional Verification
steps.instructions:
After any corrective action, perform a final controlled test:

Confirm normal monitor startup.
Confirm live image display from a compatible source.
Save a test still image.
Save a short test video when applicable.
Confirm playback on the monitor.
Transfer one saved test file to an approved USB drive.
Confirm successful export according to facility workflow.

Remove test files according to department policy and ensure no patient information remains on temporary test media.

Expected outcome: Image documentation and USB export operate normally.

If all verification steps pass: Return the monitor to service according to Clinical Engineering policy and document the completed testing.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Karl Storz C-MAC HD Monitor displayed the live laryngoscope image but would not save images or transfer documentation to USB.
CCR.Cause: Confirmed live image operation; installed SD card was not recognized during test capture, and documentation function returned after replacement with a known-good approved SD card.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the suspect SD card from use, verified test image and video capture, confirmed playback and USB export, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Monitor powers on and displays live image.
Connected scope or imager used during testing.
SD card present and fully seated.
SD card physically damaged, write-protected, full, or not recognized.
USB flash drive recognized or not recognized.
Any visible SD slot or USB port damage.
Presence of patient data requiring secure handling.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.
